item.content:encoded: This was a lot of build-up with no pay-off, and none of the imagery was particularly disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Test 5: Take a tree that is genetically similar/related to SCP-4521, and [REDACTED].

Outcome: [REDACTED BY REQUEST OF ETHICS COMMITTEE]. No screaming detected.

I tell many nooby writers that there are very few gray areas in which redactions actually add to a skip. This? This is one. Not because I am left wondering what they did to the tree — no, that's one of the reasons I dislike redactions. If you have a cool spooky thing you want them to do to the tree, write it. No, the reason why this is great is different.

It implies that this skip is giving them a sort of dissonance. They have a tree, and they are trying to make it scream. This in and of itself is ridiculous as we know, but it's telling us that they think the tree not only is capable of screaming (as all trees should be, duh) but also capable of feeling. We are trying to make it scared. And in doing so, we are humanizing the other trees. The skip makes you want to fuck up this one tree, but along the way, you still have empathy, an empathy that is deeprooted enough that even while you are horribly mutilating these tree-people you perceive in an attempt to psychologically damage the SCP, you have this nagging feeling that what you are doing is wrong. Wrong enough that the Ethics Committee, under the SCP's sway, felt the need to redact this horrible deed that they did to a tree.

This sold the article for me. I can only imagine the researchers staying up late, haunted by what they did to that poor poor douglas fir, all the while knowing it had to be done. For the greater good. Get that fucking tree to scream. To scream. To scream.

I wonder if the redaction is because that tree screamed.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the tree still have the mouth carved into it? It never said it dissipated. This is very good, the interview sold it, I wonder if the researcher actually beat up that kid? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thinks object is unsettling in a good way. It also makes you ask strange questions. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is terrifying in a very unusual way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus fucking christ what the fuck i'm actually terrified by this
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to believe the scream was some kind of hoax and the foundation just believed it (even though it wouldn't fit them, but whatever), and thay interview left me as: what the fuck?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: theme not that spot on but damn i super love this one for how silly it is. fuck those trees up, hyeah. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus Westrin, what the fuck did I just read? +1 with great enthusiasm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really weird, but very good.

+ Spoiler
– hide block

The fact it implies all trees have the ability to scream is a lot more scary than the SCP itself. While I might not classify it as a shaggy dog story, I definitely classify it as a good story.


+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wasn't feeling much until that interview, what the fuck? 1+

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
No screaming detected.

So this is for day one of jamcon. I posted a draft and critiques forum post an hour ago because my dumbass thought it wouldn't start until 12 AM (for context, I use CST, 11 PM is 12 AM EST) so I guess I'm posting it lukewarm. Also I have never wrote a shaggy dog story before so I have no idea how this'll go.

Thanks to Petrocchi, Ellie3 and JackalRelated for looking at it before it went up.

Art

You Need To Scream
by Pastarasta1
